dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 3 for priority 1 cardiac arrest with RFD-no delay in response.
Hi, I'm Alex, an EMT with the rescue squad. What made you call 911 today?
We think someone is in cardiac arrest.
Did anyone see when it happened?
We found him on the floor here in room 210. He wasn't breathing and had no pulse.
I don't think he's responsive. He's unresponsive.
Eyes are open. Pupils are 4 millimeters and non-reactive.
No verbal response to questions or voice commands. No gag reflex detected.
No motor response either. He's completely unresponsive. No limb movement with painful stimuli.
He is A&O x 0. Eye = 1, Verbal = 1, Motor = 1. GCS is 3.
We're going to do a couple of things at once. We need to secure the airway.
Setting up a BVM for ventilations. I've assessed the airway as patent but compromised. No blockage present, but no spontaneous breathing.
BVM is on now. Administering assisted ventilations at 25 LPM. Still no breath sounds detected.
No pulse at the radial or carotid artery. We will start CPR immediately.
Spontaneous respirations at 0 initially, then 10 breaths per minute with assisted ventilations.
No blood pressure detected, baseline 0/0.
No pulse present at all.
Initial SpO2 was 0, then 77%.
First EKG showed PEA rhythm, now confirmed as asystole.
PEA rhythm was initially observed; now asystolic.
Glucose initially at 0 but later measured at 29 mg/dL.
Inserting an oropharyngeal airway now.
Initiating manual CPR in two-minute rounds, rotating with another provider.
Switching to Autopulse for more consistent compressions as per protocol.
Confirmed endotracheal tube placement with capnography. Breath sounds are equal and bilateral.
Any available patient name or age from the scene?
We believe it's a 69-year-old male patient. Medical history includes congestive heart failure.
When was the last time he was seen normal?
He was seen normal at 0900 AM. We found him unresponsive at 1058 AM.
Any known pain in the patient?
No, the patient's pain score is 0 on a 10-point scale.
Skin initially warm and dry; later becomes cold, pale, and dry. Later turns cyanotic.
Pupils fixed and equal at 4 mm. No signs of neck trauma or obstruction.
Following cardiac arrest protocol. No shockable rhythm present, so we will proceed with epi and IO line per protocol. Protocol being followed is general - cardiac arrest (protocol 2 - 7).
Intraosseous access is being placed in the left humeral head. Successfully established.
Giving normal saline with pressure bag. Bolus now in progress.
Respirations remain at 10 per minute.
Still no blood pressure. Reading at 0/0.
EKG rhythm is asystole with no changes over rounds.
Placing 4-lead ECG now, per protocol.
Initiating first round of 1:10,000 epinephrine via intraosseous route.
SpO2 dropped to 74% after the first dose of epi.
Second dose of epinephrine administered as per protocol.
No changes in response or pulse. Still no signs of return of spontaneous circulation.
SpO2 is back to 80%. Continuous asystole on EKG.
Still no change; patient pain rating at 0. No new complaints or signs of distress.
Should the patient be transported?
Per on-site RN, we should continue per protocol. Transport is not needed at this time.
Second epi dose given. No return of pulse or spontaneous breathing. Continuing CPR as per protocol.
Respirations stable at 10 per minute. SpO2 intermittently at 80%.
Third round of epinephrine delivered IO.
Still no ROSC. Patient becomes colder and skin appears more pale.
EKG remains in asystole. SpO2 has dropped again to 83%.
Any known allergies or medications from the chart?
No allergies noted. Patient has CHF and no medications at the facility.
Skin is now very dry and pale. No visible rashes or bites. Later becomes highly cyanotic.
Head is intact without signs of trauma. No JVD or tracheal deviation present.
Glucose now reads 29 mg/dL. Well below hypoglycemic range, but appears consistent with current metabolic state.
SpO2 has dropped to 44%.
Calling [COMPANY_NAME] for confirmation of no transport due to asystole.
RN on scene confirms no transport needed. Establishing DC status per protocols.
Respirations 10 per minute, SpO2 34%, and EKG still asystole.
DC confirmed at 11:31 AM by [CITY] per Dr. Mason's orders.
Patient remains on the floor in room 210 with IO and ET tube secured. No transport performed.
No intervention devices left. Patient is marked as expired. Documentation is complete and verified.
